title: Throwing a lidded honey pot
subtitle: A wheel-thrown stoneware pot with a fitted lid and knob
excerpt: A wheel-thrown stoneware honey pot with a fitted lid thrown as a separate piece. The pot body has a gallery (a small recessed ledge inside the rim) that the lid sits into. The lid is thrown upside-down and trimmed to fit the gallery diameter measured with calipers.
sourceNotes: Lidded thrown pot technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A recessed ledge or shoulder formed inside the rim of a pot to seat the lid. The gallery keeps the lid from sliding sideways.
Sprung-arm measuring tool for transferring a diameter measurement from one piece to another — essential when throwing a lid to fit a pot.
A clay drying stage where the piece is firm enough to trim and join without distorting, but still slightly damp.
A trimmed ring on the underside of a thrown vessel that raises it off the surface and gives the base a refined finish.

content.text: A lidded pot requires that two separately thrown pieces (pot and lid) be made to fit each other. The key measurement is the
gallery
diameter inside the pot rim. This measurement is taken with
calipers
before the pot is cut off and used directly to set the diameter of the lid during throwing.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when trimming leather-hard stoneware. Trimming generates fine silica-bearing dust. Fire kilns in a well-ventilated space away from living areas.

items.name: Stoneware clay, smooth (for lid and knob)

content.text: Steps
Throw the pot body and form a gallery ledge inside the rim with the index finger.
Measure the gallery inner diameter with calipers and record it.
Throw the lid upside-down and pull the flange to match the caliper measurement.
Trim both pieces when leather-hard and check the lid fits the gallery.
Throwing the pot body

Centre and open the 500 g body clay, drawing up a squat cylinder 90 mm tall and 100 mm in diameter. Form the gallery in the last two throwing passes: use the side of the index finger inside the rim to push the rim wall slightly inward, creating a ledge 10 mm below the rim top and 8-10 mm wide. The gallery inner diameter should be about 70 mm. Set the calipers to this gallery diameter and record the measurement. Cut off and move to a drying board.
Throwing the lid

Centre 180 g of clay on a fresh bat. Throw the lid upside-down: open outward to a wide, flat disc 85-90 mm in diameter. Pull the rim upward slightly to form the lid flange that will drop into the gallery. Check the flange diameter against the calipers. It should match the gallery inner diameter. This is the critical measurement: too loose and the lid rattles; too tight and it will not seat after glazing. Throw a small knob from the remaining clay at the centre of the disc.
Trimming when leather-hard
When both pot and lid are
leather-hard
, trim the pot body's
foot ring

with the loop tool. Wear the N95 mask. Invert the lid on the wheel (the knob sits in a soft clay pad or in a chuck) and trim the top surface flat. Check the fit of the lid in the gallery; trim the flange slightly if needed. The lid should drop in smoothly with 1-2 mm clearance per side.
Variations
Use the same gallery technique for a storage jar or a teapot. Any lidded form uses the same caliper method.
Pull a tapered knob with a rounded top from a separate 20 g ball and score-and-slip it onto the lid centre rather than throwing it in one piece.

items.symptom: Lid does not seat properly after bisque firing
items.cause: Glazing added thickness to the flange and the fit became too tight
items.fix: Throw the lid flange 1-2 mm smaller than the gallery measurement to allow for glaze thickness on both surfaces

items.cause: Gallery formed too early when walls are still soft and unsupported
items.fix: Form the gallery in the last pass only, when the wall is fully established. Support the outside of the rim wall with the outer hand while the inside finger forms the gallery
